question: Deep technical investigation of Descript's open-sourced neural audio codec (DAC). Cover: identification, FFmpeg integration status, best current implementations, limitations/benefits/caveats, and applicability to a real-time mesh-networked audio system (mjolnir-mesh, Rust, Opus over QUIC datagrams, neural PLC pipeline in progress).
executive_summary: DAC is Kumar et al. (Descript, NeurIPS 2023) 'High-Fidelity Audio Compression with Improved RVQGAN' — MIT-licensed code AND weights at github.com/descriptinc/descript-audio-codec. It is an excellent OFFLINE neural codec (~8 kbps @ 44.1 kHz, reportedly beating EnCodec/Lyra/Opus on MUSHRA/ViSQOL) but is non-causal with ~190 ms algorithmic delay, has no FFmpeg integration, no Rust port, no streaming mode, and no PLC. The user's 'FFmpeg was implementing it' recollection is a misattribution — most likely confused with FFmpeg ticket #9194 for Google Lyra or with Descript's unrelated FFmpeg fork for MP4 seek fixes. For mjolnir-mesh, DAC is disqualified: ~190 ms delay alone exceeds the 100 ms RTT budget. Surprising finding: the repo's own docs/architecture/neural-bridge-plc.md already independently selected Mimi (Kyutai, causal, 80 ms, Apache 2.0, official Rust crate) as the correct neural codec — DAC is not mentioned.

key_findings.finding: DAC = Kumar et al. 2023 'High-Fidelity Audio Compression with Improved RVQGAN' (NeurIPS 2023 spotlight); MIT-licensed for both code and weights with fully public training data.
No FFmpeg integration exists for DAC; libavcodec/codec_id.h has zero neural codec entries; descriptinc/ffmpeg fork is for MP4 seek fixes only; FFmpeg ticket #9194 is for Google Lyra, not DAC.

Best current implementations are the official PyTorch CLI (file-based), HuggingFace transformers.DacModel (PyTorch-only, no streaming), and a 16 kHz-only ONNX export (opset 14) on HuggingFace. No Rust, no C/C++ port, no GGML/GGUF, no streaming runtime.

DAC is non-causal with ~190 ms algorithmic delay (Müller et al. Interspeech 2024, source-verified in dac/model/dac.py); chunked inference is broken (issue #39); streaming feature request (#101) unanswered since Jan 2025.

The real comparison space for low-latency lossy networks is Opus 1.5 + DRED (incumbent baseline), Mimi (only neural codec with first-class Rust), and Lyra v2 (stale since Dec 2022). EnCodec weights are CC-BY-NC (commercial blocker). 'DAC vs Opus' is a false dichotomy.

DAC is disqualified as both transport codec AND PLC substrate for mjolnir-mesh: ~190 ms algorithmic delay alone exceeds the 100 ms RTT budget; the PLC predictor's hidden state requires running the non-causal encoder live on received frames, which is architecturally impossible.

The mjolnir-mesh repo's existing architecture document docs/architecture/neural-bridge-plc.md independently selected Mimi (Kyutai, 12.5 Hz token rate, 8 codebooks, ~80 ms causal, Apache 2.0, official Rust crate, 79.3 M params) as the reference codec for token-level PLC. DAC is not mentioned. The Recover trait and PlcFactory plug points are already in place to host a Mimi-based backend.

Opus 1.5 + DRED is materially harder to beat than it appears: each 20 ms packet carries up to 1.04 s (50 frames) of RDO-VAE-encoded redundancy at < 32 kbps total overhead; outperforms LBRR and standalone Deep PLC even at 18.4% loss with 1 s bursts. Directly applicable to QUIC datagram transport.

open_questions: Mimi encoder/decoder per-frame CPU latency on a single x86 core — the most important number for committing to a Mimi-token predictor path.
Whether the moshi Rust crate exposes Mimi encoder/decoder as separable modules usable outside the Moshi pipeline, and whether they can be wrapped in tract or ort.
tPLCnet GRU-reset survival through TFLite → ONNX → tract (one-hour spike to validate the near-term TractPlc slot).
DRED jitter-buffer latency under realistic QUIC datagram burst loss — highest-leverage near-term win because it needs no neural inference.
Whether TractPlc can host a Mimi-token predictor at all — Mamba/SSM or 50–300 M-param transformer likely exceeds tract's efficient op coverage; ort may be required.
Reconciling Opus's 50 Hz frame cadence with Mimi's 12.5 Hz token rate (4:1 downsampling or different concealment granularity).
DAC's active maintenance status post-Adobe departure of first author — affects durability of even the offline reference role.
Exact parameter count of DAC 44 kHz (community-sourced ~74M vs official-tag 76.6M).
CPU RTF on x86 and Apple Silicon for DAC 16 kHz (smallest variant).
